name: Ralph Loop
description: Autonomous stateless-loop methodology (Geoffrey Huntley) in which an AI coding agent re-reads specs and iterates one task per cycle with fresh context, typically driven by a minimal bash loop.

description.value: Stateless agent-loop pattern (`while :; do cat PROMPT.md | claude ; done`) in which a fresh agent context picks one task per iteration from on-disk state (specs, plan, AGENTS.md) until the spec is satisfied.

website.comment: Canonical write-up is Huntley's July 2025 blog post; the GitHub repo is the companion playbook.

first-release.comment: Methodology introduced in Huntley's blog post dated 2025-07-14; companion repo published shortly after.

latest-release.comment: No tagged releases; latest commit pushed 2026-01-11. Verified 2026-04-21.

license.comment: Repository has no LICENSE file; GitHub API reports no license. Strictly all-rights-reserved by default, though the README content is widely shared and adapted.

pricing-model.comment: Free to read and adopt; no commercial offering. Note license ambiguity above — treated as a free pattern in practice.

open-source.comment: Public repo but no OSI-approved license declared, so does not meet the OSI definition of open source despite being freely available.

covered-phases.comment: Specs in specs/*.md (spec); PROMPT_plan produces IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md (planning + tasks); PROMPT_build picks tasks and implements (implementation); 'backpressure' phase runs tests/builds (testing). Self-evaluation/loop-back is a step but produces no distinct artifact, so not tagged as review.

methodology-style.comment: Value lives in the loop+phase discipline (process-centric); planning prompt and building prompt are explicitly separate (plan-and-execute); specs/ folder is the source of truth code is generated from (spec-driven). Not tagged multi-agent — Ralph runs one agent per loop iteration; subagent fan-out is a Claude Code feature, not a multi-agent role split.

opinionated.comment: Prescribes one-task-per-loop, deterministic file set per iteration, fresh context every cycle, no placeholder code, ripgrep-before-implement — a tight set of rules.

ceremony-level.comment: Four files plus a bash one-liner; no ADRs, change logs, release notes, or formal review gates.

mechanization-level.comment: loop.sh runs the agent end-to-end without human stepping, but the methodology requires the user to author PROMPT.md, AGENTS.md, and specs by hand and to tune the prompts as failures emerge — not a turnkey pipeline.

hitl-checkpoints.comment: Default loop runs without checkpoints; Huntley caveats that experienced operator oversight is needed in practice but the methodology defines no explicit gates.

approval-gates.comment: No phase-level approval gates by design.

autonomous-e2e.comment: Entire premise: the loop runs to completion (or budget exhaustion) without intervention.

autonomy-level.comment: Default configuration is fully autonomous — the bash loop only stops on Ctrl-C or completion.

rollback-support.comment: No built-in rollback; relies on the user reverting via git after the fact.

supported-agents.comment: All worked examples and prompt files target Claude Code (`claude --dangerously-skip-permissions`). README explicitly notes the same approach 'can be used with other CLIs; e.g. amp, codex, opencode' but provides no working examples for them.

editor-integrations.comment: Methodology is a shell loop driving an agent CLI; no editor plugin.

model-agnostic.comment: The loop pattern is independent of any specific model — any agent CLI that accepts a prompt over stdin works. Huntley's worked examples use Claude (Sonnet/Opus) but he names Gemini as a fallback for large error sets.

mcp-support.comment: No MCP integration; Ralph predates and ignores MCP. Underlying agent (e.g., Claude Code) may use MCP independently.

artifact-formats.comment: All non-code artifacts (PROMPT.md, AGENTS.md, IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md, specs/*.md) are Markdown.

artifacts-covered.comment: specs/*.md (specs), IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md doubles as plan + prioritized task list. AGENTS.md is operator guidance, not classified as one of the listed artifact kinds.

project-layout.value: Repo root holds PROMPT.md (or PROMPT_plan.md / PROMPT_build.md), AGENTS.md, IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md, loop.sh, plus specs/*.md and src/ for the application code.

artifacts-in-git.comment: All state files live in the repo and are committed alongside source code; that on-disk persistence is the entire point.

language-agnostic.comment: Methodology has no language assumption; Huntley's flagship example built a Rust+LLVM compiler, and community ports cover other stacks.

enforcement-mechanisms.comment: Process discipline is enforced through PROMPT.md and AGENTS.md instructions only (instructional). The 'backpressure' phase requires the agent to run tests/builds and self-correct on failure (test-gates).

strict-phase-ordering.comment: User chooses between PROMPT_plan.md and PROMPT_build.md by swapping which file the loop pipes; no mechanism prevents running build before plan.

artifact-validation.comment: No schema or validator for the markdown artifacts.

memory-implementation.comment: Persistent state is exclusively in markdown files (IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md, AGENTS.md, specs/) plus git history; no vector DB or knowledge graph.

persistent-memory.comment: On-disk files are the entire point — they survive between agent invocations and are how the loop preserves progress across fresh contexts.

project-context-files.comment: AGENTS.md is described as the 'single, canonical heart of the loop' — a project-scoped operator/build guide for the agent.

cost-tracking.comment: No built-in token or cost tracking in the methodology; any tracking comes from the underlying agent CLI.

run-history.comment: No structured trace store; per-iteration commits to git are the only durable record.

telemetry-opt.comment: The loop itself collects no telemetry — there is nothing to opt out of. Underlying agent CLIs may have their own telemetry policies.

custom-templates.comment: The user authors PROMPT.md and AGENTS.md from scratch; the entire methodology IS user-supplied templates.

hooks.comment: No lifecycle hook system; the bash loop is the only extension point.

sub-agents.comment: Methodology explicitly advocates fanning out to many parallel subagents (up to ~500 Sonnet workers) for search/analysis. Subagent definition reuses the underlying agent's mechanism (Claude Code's Task tool).

marketplace.comment: No central template marketplace, though many community forks and adaptations exist (e.g. ralph-orchestrator, ralph-playbook, awesome-ralph).

install-mechanism.comment: Clone the repo or copy the files manually; the loop itself is a one-line shell script (loop.sh).

setup-time.comment: Mechanically: clone, chmod +x loop.sh, run. Authoring useful PROMPT.md/AGENTS.md/specs for a real project takes far longer, but the methodology's setup is trivial.

self-hosted.comment: Runs entirely on the user's machine; no hosted Ralph service.

requires-account.comment: The methodology itself requires no account. Underlying agent (e.g., Claude Code) requires its own vendor account, but that's the agent runtime, treated as out of scope per RESEARCH.md.

git-worktree-support.comment: No mention of git worktrees in the README or blog; the methodology assumes a single working tree.

documentation-quality.comment: Authoritative blog post plus a single README with worked example files. Adequate for the small surface area but no formal docs site or reference material; tone is conversational rather than reference-grade.

github-stars.comment: Captured from GitHub API on 2026-04-21. Stars stale quickly.

active-maintenance.comment: Latest commit 2026-01-11, ~100 days before research date 2026-04-21 — just past the 90-day RESEARCH.md threshold. The methodology is stable rather than abandoned; community ports remain active.

example-projects.comment: Huntley's CURSED self-hosting language compiler is the flagship documented case study; the playbook repo itself is also a worked example.

community-channel.comment: No official Discord/Slack/forum. Discussion happens informally on X/Twitter and across community forks; nothing first-party.
